sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A lab constructed parallel-plate capacitors with plate area A = 2.0 × 10⁻³ m² and varied plate separation d. With air between the plates, measured capacitances at separations of 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, and 8.0 mm were approximately 17.7, 8.9, 4.4, and 2.2 pF. Students then inserted a dielectric into the 2.0-mm capacitor and observed that capacitance increased.
questions.stem: What capacitance is shown at a 2.0-mm separation?

explanations.C: Correct. The figure gives approximately 8.9 pF at d = 2.0 mm. This is half the capacitance at 1.0 mm because capacitance is inversely proportional to plate separation.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Capacitance decreases as parallel-plate separation increases.

explanations.A: Correct. For a parallel-plate capacitor, C = εA/d. With area and dielectric constant fixed, increasing separation decreases capacitance.
explanations.B: qvB is the magnetic force expression, not capacitance.
explanations.C: IR relates voltage to current and resistance, not capacitance.
explanations.D: hf gives photon energy, not capacitance.

questions.stem: What would happen to capacitance if plate area were doubled at constant separation?

explanations.D: Correct. Capacitance is directly proportional to plate area in C = εA/d. Doubling A doubles C when separation and dielectric are constant.
explanations.A: Halving would occur if separation doubled, not if area doubled.
explanations.B: Area appears directly in the equation, so capacitance would change.
explanations.C: The relationship is linear in area, not squared.

questions.stem: Why does inserting a dielectric increase capacitance?

choices.B: The dielectric reduces the effective electric field for a given free charge, allowing more charge storage at the same voltage
choices.C: The dielectric eliminates all electric field lines
choices.D: The dielectric converts capacitance into resistance

explanations.B: Correct. A dielectric polarizes in the field, reducing the effective field and voltage for a given charge. This increases C = Q/V.
explanations.A: The passage keeps the same physical separation when the dielectric is inserted.
explanations.C: A dielectric reduces the effective field but does not eliminate all field lines.
explanations.D: Capacitance and resistance are distinct circuit properties.

questions.stem: If the 2.0-mm capacitor is charged to 5.0 V, approximately how much charge is stored without the dielectric?

explanations.C: Correct. Using Q = CV with C = 8.9 pF and V = 5.0 V gives Q = 44.5 pC. The unit prefix matters because pF × V gives pC.
explanations.A: 1.8 pC would result from dividing capacitance by voltage rather than multiplying.
explanations.B: 8.9 C ignores both the voltage and the pico prefix.
explanations.D: 44.5 C has the right number but wrong prefix by a factor of 10¹².

questions.passage: A simplified mass spectrometer accelerates singly charged ions through the same potential difference and then bends them in a uniform magnetic field. Three ions enter the magnetic region with the same charge but different masses. Their measured radii of curvature are 4.0, 5.7, and 8.0 cm. Students use r = mv/(qB) and note that larger radius indicates larger momentum when q and B are fixed.
questions.stem: Which ion has the largest radius of curvature?

choices.C: All ions have the same radius

explanations.D: Correct. Ion C has the largest bar at 8.0 cm. Larger radius corresponds to a less tightly curved path in the magnetic field.
explanations.A: Ion A has the smallest radius at 4.0 cm.
explanations.B: Ion B is intermediate at 5.7 cm.
explanations.C: The plotted radii are not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Measured curvature radius for three singly charged ions in the same magnetic field.

questions.stem: For fixed q and B, what does a larger radius imply?

choices.D: Lower magnetic field strength in that trial

explanations.A: Correct. From r = mv/(qB), radius is directly proportional to momentum when q and B are fixed. Thus a larger radius indicates larger mv.
explanations.B: A smaller momentum would produce a smaller radius.
explanations.C: The magnetic force is not zero; it provides the centripetal force.
explanations.D: The passage states that the same magnetic field is used.

questions.stem: What is the direction of the magnetic force relative to the ion velocity?

choices.D: In the same direction as the electric potential

explanations.B: Correct. Magnetic force on a moving charge is qvB sin θ and is perpendicular to the velocity and magnetic field. This perpendicular force curves the path without directly changing speed.
explanations.A: A force parallel to velocity would change speed rather than produce circular curvature.
explanations.C: The force is not simply opposite velocity; it depends on v × B.
explanations.D: Electric potential is not a direction of magnetic force.

questions.stem: If the magnetic field strength were doubled, what would happen to the radius for the same ion speed?

choices.C: It would be cut in half

explanations.C: Correct. The equation r = mv/(qB) shows radius is inversely proportional to B. Doubling B halves r.
explanations.A: Doubling would occur if momentum doubled, not if B doubled.
explanations.B: B appears directly in the denominator, so radius changes.
explanations.D: Quadrupling is not supported by the inverse first-power dependence.

questions.stem: Why can the magnetic field separate ions with different mass-to-charge ratios?

choices.A: Different m/q values produce different curvature radii under the same field conditions
choices.B: All ions travel in straight lines in a magnetic field
choices.C: The magnetic force acts only on neutral particles
choices.D: Mass-to-charge ratio has no effect on circular motion

explanations.A: Correct. The radius depends on mass, velocity, charge, and field. Under controlled acceleration and magnetic field, ions with different mass-to-charge ratios follow paths with different radii.
explanations.B: Charged ions curve in a magnetic field rather than travel straight, unless the force is zero.
explanations.C: Magnetic force acts on moving charged particles, not neutral particles.
explanations.D: The data show that different ions have different radii, and m/q is central to that separation.
